---
title: ABI
description: A guide in my new Starlight docs site.
---

Application Binary Interface (ABI) is a low-level interface defined in ARC-4 between an application and its caller.

It defines how to:
- describe the properties of an application,
- describe, select, and invoke a method in an application, and
- encode the arguments and decode the return value of an application method.

Any application that adheres to this standard is called an ARC-4 Application.
The ABI standard is what allows typed client to be produced starting from an application definition.
This applications definition is a JSON file containing the information talked about in this page.

There are two ARC standards that have since extended ARC-4 in a backwards-compatible way:
ARC-32, and ARC-56.

## Methods
Application Call Transactions always execute the same body of code
(your approval or clear program).
However, we often want an Application to be able to execute distinct actions
(mutate state, move funds, etc.).

We call these distinct actions methods and the ABI provides specifications for building
an Application Call, or other higher-level abstractions, that invokes each specific Application method.

A method is completely defined by its: name, arguments, and return value.
Example: `add(uint64,uint64)uint128`.

### Method selectors
A method selector is a 4-bytes string derived from the SHA-512/256 of the method string.
Therefore, each method is associated deterministically with a method selector.

When an Application Call Transaction is built, the method selector is the first element of the Arguments Array.
ARC-4 does not explicitly make recommendations on how to route the code execution to the selected method and in fact,
different languages implement different (all equivalent) method routing strategies.

## OnCompletion Actions
Any Application Call Transaction specifies a field called `OnCompletion`.
Depending on the value of this field, a successful execution of this transaction will have a specific side effect:
- (NoOp) No side effect.
- (DeleteApplication) self-explanatory.
- (UpdateApplication) self-explanatory.
- (OptIn) locks a portion of the caller's balance to allocate space on the application's local storage.
- (CloseOut) removes the caller's local state from the Application and unlocks the same portion of balance.
- (ClearState) same as CloseOut. It will have the same side effects, but it cannot be rejected.

A method can elect to be used for all or some of these `OnCompletion` values except for Clear state.
ClearState should be considered either a call to free an account from a faulty Application, or an adversarial call.

## Data Types
Natively, the AVM only supports uint64 and byte slices.
The ABI defines abstract data types like: numbers, boolean, strings, tuples, arrays, and more.
These types are built on top of native byte slices and can be combined with each other
(example: `Tuple[uint8,string,array[uint64]]`).
